title: James Abela: The high-stakes search for Quality

segments.text: Today on the rules of investing, I'm speaking with James Abila, portfolio manager at Fidelity
International. James is a code portfolio manager on Fidelity's future leaders and global
future leaders funds, giving him a unique perspective across both Australian and global
markets. Today, we'll be drawing on insights from Fidelity's team of 180 analysts located
across the world. We'll also explore the underperformance of quality stocks, which have
endured their toughest period of underperformance since the lead up to the GFC. But these
periods don't last forever. So stick around to hear about the compelling ideas James has
uncovered in his search for future leaders on the ASX and abroad. My name is James Marley
and this podcast is brought to you by live-wire markets. Now, James, thanks for coming in.
Great to catch up and good to see you. Thanks very much James, good to see you again.
Now we've spoken many times over the years. I've thrown lots of questions at you, but
I thought maybe as a bit of an icebreaker, what's a question that you think people in
my position should be asking you but we never do? What's the one question you wish we'd
ask you and what's your response? I think probably just what gets you up in the morning,
which is like financial markets, that is my world, but just seeing humanity over
achieve and achieve and innovate and change, that is what really excites me about actually
being in financial markets to see things domestically and globally that literally change
the world and being in finance, you have the benefit of seeing the future of the world
through the eyes of business people that are innovating in product. And if you extend
that out of finance into music or other areas of the human endeavour, which change in
innovating, create things that are just phenomenal, I mean that's what really excites me in
the world as a human being and I think financial markets is an amazing place because you get
to see that frontier and that's really I guess an interesting question that I've never
really been asked. Are you innately an optimist? Because that's quite a, I like that, that's
an optimistic way to kick off the podcast. Yes, definitely. I'm up at the mystic and I do see
the positive side of the world generally. The world does go through tumultuous times. I think
we are in one now where things are quite influx and there's a lot of uncertainty, but generally
I am quite optimistic and generally society, humanity and financial markets get through it
pretty much as a group. We've been through the GFC, we've been through the tech crash,
so there's been a lot of things that have been thrown at the world, whether it's risk or
COVID which is a pandemic which is phenomenal, even my kids are saying my kids will not believe,
we were locked up in the house for six months, they will just not believe it. So there is amazing
things that the humanity has gone through and so I think I am optimistic and I think you need
to be because we do go through a lot of things but it is generally just a positive outcome of the
end. Okay, I like that as a way to start the podcast because as we were talking about just
before we kicked off, for me personally I find myself a little out to see, usually I run quite
bullish, I have conviction in markets right now, it feels like a difficult time for me at least
thinking about where I would allocate money and where I have conviction.
At Fidelity as I mentioned in the introduction, you have 180 analysts there positioned around the
world, sector specialists, which is an incredible resource for you to draw on. Given the
environment that we find ourselves in, you alluded to it being a bit challenging as well,
I thought you could perhaps draw on some of the information that you've gained from that
network of analysts to help give you a bit of clarity on some of the views that you're forming
about where we sit today and how that flows onto equity markets. Yeah James, definitely look,
the big theme globally and I'll just start global to come back to Australia, it really is about AI.
So there's AI winners and AI, I guess leadership in the marketplace so that's very broad,
semiconductors, cabling, engineering, physical build, plus all the hyperscalers as well.
And then you've got really the non-AI companies and the non-AI companies are those that are not
affected by AI. I guess it's either physical, trust, something that's very embedded.
And that is really a good place to be and when we own a lot of stocks in that area.
In the middle of the rump is the AI losers and that's becoming quite a broad spectrum of the market,
which are software companies, engineering companies, service companies that are affected and
threatened by the revolution or evolution of AI and what its capabilities are.
That's really what the global setting is. So in Europe,
multiples of compressed and you've got a mix of companies, the US is definitely where the AI
leadership is really taking place. So the extremes of AI leadership and then AI losers is probably
the highest in that market. Japan, you do get some exposure through peripherals, through technology,
through other innovations that are related like robotics or automation.
And when you come back to the Australian market, it's really resources, to be honest,
is really where the powerhouse of the Australian market is occurring right now. That's where you're
getting a lot of shortness or concern about supply. So commodities are going well.
And then you do have those, still those non-AI winners that are still doing quite well.
So that's really, I guess, two areas in the market, which are still quite strong.
But globally, it is a very difficult environment because of the factors of basically
got inflation, you've got AI, you've got consumer concerns, you've got significant regulation
and you've got geopolitical uncertainty right now. So just with those five factors,
it is quite hard to have very high conviction in certain things unless it's very, very clear right
now. You touched on it there, high inflation. This is a burning topic here in Australia.
We're in a different position to other parts of the world. How are you thinking about
the rates and inflation environment and its flow on impact equities, which is your bread and butter?
Yeah, it's a very good question. What it does is increases the hurdle. So companies that are
a bit more marginal in terms of cost of capital, it becomes a lot harder. Companies that are leveraged
also, it becomes harder because again, that cost of capital is going up. So that becomes more
challenging. So what you need to have is companies that aren't heavily leveraged,
that do have some pricing power is the key. So pricing power and ability to basically
price in that inflation is really, really important. If you're in markets that are very competitive,
if you're in markets that are a lack of pricing power, or there is a lot of competition,
it becomes very, very difficult to survive in a market where there is high inflation.
Then if you have an industry which is very leveraged, also it becomes difficult. So something
in finance or banking, it can also become very difficult. If you couple that with in Australia,
the tax changes, I think that puts quite a squeeze on companies, especially where there is
financial leverage, especially like housing in the Australian market, it's going to soften
or it's going to slow down in terms of its growth trajectory, that's the general perception.
That makes the mix of inflation and also that driver, which has been really strong in terms of
confidence for the Australian market, it just becomes a lot harder. When the interesting thing on
that point of companies that have the ability to pass on price rises, a number of those
companies in the domestic market at least have effectively had a question mark put over their
ability to pass through those price rises. You think about companies like RIA Group or Zero,
whatever it might be. They've got a new competitor and there's less places to go find that it
appears. Yeah, definitely. I think software and consumer are the two where it's hit really hard.
Software has definitely been because of AI as well. It's been a really difficult place to get
pricing power and on the consumer side because of consumer confidence has been a bit weaker.
That's also been hard. So definitely those two areas were already seen announcements to say it's
becoming more challenging and those stocks have been pretty weak and coming down in valuations
and a little bit in earnings but a lot of it is in valuation as well.
I'm keen to dive into the area of quality stocks which in markets we've got these labels for
factors or styles of investing. All of them sound quite appealing value. Yeah, I'd love some
value. What about some growth? Yeah, I'll take that quality who doesn't want quality.
Now you draw on all of those factors within your portfolio construction,
momentum as well. But for people that are not across the jargon, what does quality mean?
Well, I make it really, really simple and I try to do that myself. Quality,
so the return and equity of our country is around 9% to 10% return and equity. That's the profit
divided by the equity contribution is 9%. You look at the markets in the world. That ROE
correlation to total return is really high. Our country's return in terms of equities is around 9%.
It's very correlated. So quality is just a company that can manage to do two to three percent
above that level. So basically 11 to 12% sustainable ROE is what we call a quality business.
Now you want to look at that at least sort of three to five year period and quite persistent as well.
That is what the traditional definition of quality is and that's what we try to term as quality.
Now the difference is quality and momentum is what you need to really distinguish,
especially in Australian market. And momentum is where your return and equities
is high or your return your returns levels are high but it's cyclical. It's not structural or
it's not persistent. So that's the difference between quality and momentum and it's very
important to distinguish between those two things. Just on quality, it's well, it's been well
covered but you've also published some content yourselves from Fidelity. Talking about the
challenging period that the quality factor has had, I think it's the worst period since the
lead up to the GFC, you know, approaching two years of underperformance. What's behind that because
the traits that you've outlined sound innately attractive to investors, yet it has had this period
about the performance. So why is that being? So there's really two reasons. The big one is really AI.
AI has short meduration, shortness certainty and increased expectation of
capex or innovation in order to sustain leadership in whatever market it is, whether it's realistate.com
that are used to real estate in cars, in houses, but then also in engineering and in software,
you know, across the board. So AI has really shortened that duration. So that return profile,
the market is now saying it's coming down, but also the persistency and the duration of it is
also coming in and that's why multiples have come down very dramatically. That's and the bulk
of the quality stocks in the Australian market are in that software sector. The ones that are also
non-related haven't really come down significantly. So something like a Fisher and Piker,
which had a result out today and it was good, that stayed at that 40 times PE, which is more than
double the PE of the market for a sustained period because again, it's not an AI, I guess,
it's deep trust, it's physical and it's heavy innovation, which is physical innovation,
not innovation based on information or innovation based on software. So that's really why
quality has really started to underperform. It really is AI. On the other side of it is value and
when the market is macro uncertain, the market does go towards value actually and momentum.
It chooses that paradigm because it doesn't have the patience to pay for something that's 10
years away from now. It wants to have it right now. So the market goes towards value, which in this
market, in the Australian market especially has been resources and resources has really been
one of the key drivers of the market. Small cap resources last year were up 95%. That is just
massive. And the only time that's happened in history in Australia is 2016-17. Those periods
when you had the US balance sheet crisis, the Greece crisis, etc. And the market was very uncertain,
macro like it is now in the market in a very short direction. So value rally was the last one we had
and that value rally has continued since about June July of last year. And they typically do
last 12 to sometimes 18 months. Most of the return comes through in about 12 months. So quality
is probably still going to have another 5 or 6 months of quite typical time. And that coincides
with AI that people won't really know what actually the impacts of AI actually will be. And you'll
start to see them. You're already seeing them globally a lot of redundancies because of AI.
And you're starting to see the impacts of that. So if you start to see earnings weaken or
capex going up or visibility of these businesses that had 5 to 10 years visibility, it's now
2 or 3 years visibility, that's going to really cause concern for those quality stocks. But those
that can deliver are going to start to re-rate. As I mentioned, the Fisher and PICL has and there
are examples of that in the US market as well. So that's what I was going to ask you in terms of
a catalyst for a turnaround in quality. Are you simply following earnings update to earnings
update to see how material the impact of AI is on these companies and therefore it's a 12 to 18
month process? Yes, agreed. And it's going to take time for that reality to start to hit.
People say the analogy is newspapers. Newspapers are fine for about three or four years after we
all started to read news on our phones. And then everything just clift like 80 or 90 percent.
So people are expecting in a 12 to 18 months you just to get some sort of idea of change.
But I do think it is a 12 to 18 month process. And this is in some industries it's a revolution
and in some industries it's going to be an evolution and in other industries it's just going to be
non-existent or very low impact. So there's also that breadth of three layers of impact that the
market has to distill in individual companies, each company that they're looking at. And that'll take
time. It is going to take time. So I agree with you. I think it's 12 to 18 months.
I promise listeners I'm going to take this conversation in a more upbeat direction in just a moment
because James is an optimist, I'm an optimist. But I just want to touch on periods of underperformance
and difficult times. You're paid to manage through these periods, investors, listeners,
this podcast might be less sure of how they deal with it. What have you learnt about investing
through difficult times? How do you manage it? Are there any tips for our listeners?
Look it happens to most managers and every factor has a difficult phase. You need to know I guess
why it's happening. So I try to have a very deep look at why it's happening at the portfolio level.
So that's like factors and a portfolio structure level. Then at the industry level and then at
the individual stock level. And you need to do those three levels to really understand why it's
happening. And then you need to think about what am I going to do about it? How am I going to
reposition for it? What have I learned but what has in reality changed and what is transient,
what is structural? And the biggest lesson is you can't change your process. If you've had a
process whether it is a value manager, a momentum manager or a quality manager or for me I'm a blended
manager. You need to stick to that process and reflect on your underperformance in the context of
your process. And that is what I've learned. And I've always done it in 2016-17 was my other
period of underperformance. That was a lot more severe and I probably would have been in that position
if I didn't take the lessons and actually act on change. That I looked at quality valuations
and extremes and also valuations extremes, value extremes in that market. And when they were
which it was in 2024 and 2025, quality and massively outperformed, massively outperformed.
And a lot of that was software and value which is cyclicals and resources had significantly
underperformed. And there was a very similar pattern in 2016-17 where that happened and then
as I just mentioned to you in that period 2016-17 energy and resources went up 100% during that
phase and that's what happened again in this phase just now. But that's what I was reducing
quality towards the back end of last year, trimming on the software names, trimming on very,
very high PE stocks that have 60 to 70% of their alpha or price performance was just PE re-rating.
Now that is great to emphasise to the market and the world that you have duration. But when that's
too extreme, the risks on the downside are very significant which is what's happened globally
in the last sort of six to nine months. So that's what I'd say about underperformance. So for me,
I've, yeah, I now own quite a lot of resources. But in terms of quality, I haven't gone,
you know, completed the other way. I haven't changed my process. I've trimmed down and where I am
comfortable that the companies are not, I am impacted I guess. I've actually added more
in the last six to nine months to those stocks that are still doing well, delivering good
numbers, what they're doing, you know, is not AI affected. They're still market leaders,
they're still market innovators. And that's still where I'm comfortable to keep on adding back
into quality. What are a couple of examples there? Hub is one and Fisher and Parkle is the other one.
You know, both of them in my top 10 have actually added to them. Also, Charter Hall are people
worried about that. Same as we on CBR in the Global Fund and that was worried about
that being an information services type company. But actually, it is, it's a network of investors
that are focused on fixed income. So they're the three that I've added to. I'd say quality,
Charter Hall, Harban Fisher and Parkle Healthcare. Yeah. Now, as I mentioned in my introduction,
you've got the ability to invest both globally and locally, which is, I guess, a bit of a luxury,
maybe a bit of extra work. But if we look globally, attention is all on the hyperscalers
and the AI build out. You're focused on finding future leaders, which excuse you to the
the mid and the small cap part of the market. What's the opportunity set that you're seeing and
how you're playing it? What's getting you, what's getting you excited? Yeah, I'll just frame it in
terms of market cap size. So our actual mid caps in the world is actually now 100 billion US. That's
mid caps. That's small caps down to go down to one billion. So 100 billion US is 150 billion
dollar companies. So these are big companies. But the opportunity set really has been really towards,
I guess, AI, but the breadth of AI. So semiconductors, memory, cabling, peripherals, consulting,
then you've got builders, engineering. So the breadth of actually picks and shovels in mid caps
and small caps. And it's in that 25 to 30 percent range of our funders now, AI, related.
So that's quite significant. And the opportunity set is quite large. Obviously, hyperscalers
and very large semiconductor businesses like Nvidia are out of our league and being three,
four trillion dollars market caps now. But in that 100 billion dollars and below, there is a lot of
picks and shovels businesses which have been doing incredibly well. So we do own a lot of them
right across the board. Like I said, semis and a lot of the builders are comfort systems actually
now our number one position. Comfort systems is one of the builders in terms of construction and
they've been building data centers which upgrades have been significant over the years, especially
the last two to three years. But the breadth of opportunity is significant. And is that 30 percent
exposure you said meaningful? Is that like a really big bet for you in the fund?
Well, it's spread across information technology, communication services,
utilities, or even an energy company as well. It's probably one of the largest
thematic exposures we've had when we think about our funding terms of theme exposures. It's
probably one of the largest. We've had typically we have 10 to 20 percent exposed to one theme.
But being 25 to 30 percent it's probably one of the largest themes. But the breadth of it and the
I guess diversity of that exposure is quite large. We're also not chasing very hot things. We try to
get things that have duration in them. So three to five year visibility is what we want to have
and what we want to be able to see. But it is quite a significant position. So we are
quite conscious of it. Like the fund perform very strongly the global future leads fund perform
very strongly in the month of April because of the fact that we did have a lot of AI exposure.
So that's it is symptomatic that it's doing well when AI is doing well. So we're very conscious
of that. But we have tried to create breadth rather than just having one or two kind of exposures.
It's about five or six kind of exposures that we do have in that 25 to 30 percent exposure.
Let's stay with that perhaps. You could share a recent edition on that global front.
The ideas I've looked through your top 10 companies I've never heard of that clearly as you talk
about the scale of these businesses, quite meaningful. Perhaps you could just take us on a little
bit of a stock story and talk through a recent edition, explain what the business does,
how it's involved in that AI build out, if that's the stock that you want to choose.
Yeah, well a new edition is is lamenting which is actually optical technology. So
just cabling inside AI and from the AI data center to some sort of a junction point
and also just between within the actual data centers optical cables is becoming a much bigger theme.
It's quite a well-known theme I guess globally but domestically we know about copper a lot
because we have stocks like sandfire and other copper stocks in the Australian market
but globally optical fiber technology to transmit data just because of the speed,
the demand and the volumes of data that is just going from our phones and computers into the cloud
from the cloud back into workplaces or into data centers and then back out again.
The view is that this is a new technology that allows for basically faster
more volume of data transmission and so that's something that we we're recently invested in which
is Lamentum there are others that we also own which is a really fascinating technology it's very
innovative and the growth in that market is accelerating quite rapidly because of the the speed of
data center builds but also it's just a volume and speed requirements of data in the world.
And what sort of multiple or price are you paying for a business like that?
It's not cheap so it's not like it's in the high multiples so that 30, 40 times multiple but it
is growing very rapidly what you've had in a lot of names like comfort systems is an easy one
that we'll talk about in terms of multiples that's on in a nearly 40 times multiple but the upgrade
from 12 months ago to today it's in the 70 to 80% range and it's an IPS upgrade so if expectations
one year ago were X and today they're 80% above X Lamentum similar where expectations in terms of
earnings is going up and then I like to talk about the structure of earnings so the structure
of earnings like this year next year the year after and the year after it's all upward and Lamentum
is the same you know we're not talking about I guess you know companies that are growing 5-10%
and it's kind of you know or it's or it's cyclical it's up 40% and then going downwards this is
a structural growth engine which is why I guess the multiples are quite high but expectations are rising
and the growth that's occurring is you know 30, 40, 50% in terms of earnings you know over the next
three to five years so they're in an in an environment of AI where everything is quite
exponential to be honest and that's where the earnings expectations are going up the speed of
the growth is accelerating quite quickly you know I was just listening to a podcast about Anthropics
Revenue's I mean they are hockey stick phenomenal growth and this whole environment is obviously
getting excited by that but it is it is quite real that this is you know this is happening quite
quickly and the breadth of it is what is exciting and that is what the investment opportunity breadth
is what is exciting because it it is significant and it is quick but it is quite broad okay
what sounds like you got lots to get excited about on the global front what about the Australian
market the the bread and butter of many of our listeners feels feels like we're being a little
overlooked across many sectors down here at the moment resources it's great that we've got that
big part of our economy what's the opportunity that you see here in Australia yeah definitely
resources is one of the biggest ones so yeah we have quite a lot of resources in the fun but don't
yet I think you've got to think also non-resources there is quite a lot of still good things going on
so FinTech as I mentioned companies like hub and net wealth are still doing well health care has
been a terrible time in the Australian market with large caps you Cochlear CSL have come down
enormously you're probably great grateful you can't venture into that I'm glad I'm not in large
caps I'm glad I'm not doing the large cap environment so they've been really tough so one bright spot
in my space has been Fisher and Parkle health care which has been you know doing really well
delivered a good result today and that's you know again it's not AI affected it's got a deep trust
environment it's hospitals it's critical intensive care or sleep apnea so these are in a very
critical deep trust environments which is again very important same as FinTech the FinTech businesses
being hub and net wealth this is $100 billion of capital is sitting on those platforms
being trusted by investors to be a true representation of their wealth a true representation of
what the values are and that is a system which has been you know successful still so these are
things that are still exciting me and they're still you know in my significant position and holdings
and the yeah the resources piece is significant because that is that is feeding really the AI
boom it's not just AI though because it's it's rare earths it's copper it's nickel it's gold so
the Australian resources breadth is is quite wide so it's best to be exposed to a lot of them and
also steel so there is quite a lot to be excited about in terms of resources so I'd say resources
probably the big one then I'd say it's yeah those sort of innovative leadership quality companies
that are still doing well which I mentioned chart all Fisher and Parkle and Hub they're still doing
well and then it's really the tail is of other businesses so whether it's you know
defensive or real estate I still own a small tail of other things as well but they're the main
things really resources which is mainly in momentum to be honest right now and then those
quality names which from those long duration industry leaders industry winners innovators in
technology or innovators in health care and trust that really sort of lead and drive the portfolio
at the moment yep so that Australian portfolio based on the latest numbers I saw had a 30% exposure
to materials when was the last time you had that big of an exposure yeah I think it's about 36%
today the last time that happens was that appeared that I mentioned in 2017 oh no actually was
lithium bubble lithium bubble which was 2021 22 so three or four years ago it was and that was
when you had it's very similar theme you had like one or two things which are driving the market
up but you had like maybe one or two stocks two years before the boom and then you had about 10
stocks that were part of that bubble so it was I was I'm lithium bubble driven by electric vehicles
right then and that was a huge theme and you had Pilbara you had Galaxy and you had about 10
lithium stocks and that was the last time we had but gold was doing okay then as well so it was
actually multiple kind of drivers of that of that resources boom but that was the last time we
had such a significant weight in resources we have that again now but it actually is broader now
which is quite significant for the Australian market especially means it's malls because it is
that breadth of year lithium as well as nickel as well as copper as well as gold as well as
rare earths as well as steel so that without the last time it was mainly lithium that was driving up
that bubble but it was resources and then yet 2016 17 was the last time so it was really 2016 17
then you had really lithium bubble period and then you've got it now so it's quite a lot for
that happened three times in in basically 10 years and you know luckily for the Australian market
it's quite a significant opportunity because again there's cyclicals and you've got to be willing
to change your mind that this cyclical up swing is happening you get upgrades you get multiples
commodity prices go up and they do you know they do go upwards with the earnings and when the market
is very value focused they do go short duration so they want to see the earnings now not
something in 5 or 10 years time so when the market loses that duration I guess impatience the
resources names and other names which have earnings strong now is something that is going to be
basically I guess working and performing in the market yeah Australia's large cap miners very
well-known very well owned in the mid cap space what are some of the ideas that you like
I really is lithium so Pilbara has just gone out but I still like that and can hold on to that
one so that's PLS IGO is another good name that we own they're probably two significant ones and
then you own a little bit of sand fire which is copper but those three you know quite see
you begin and then there's a range of gold names but Pilbara has been you know see you begin
position for me for a long period of time it's in a good space lithium is going very well in terms
of market structure and commodity prices and and Pilbara has been a very good manager to you know
basically to get exposure to that marketplace yeah we're going to move into our regular questions
in just a moment but when you participated in our outlook series it started a year you talked
about the momentum in growth stocks which was a big theme last year and an expectation that could
continue obviously there was a moment of realization earlier this year when that momentum was halted
is that an opportunity now in in some of those growth names whether it was that heavy sell-off
or is it just time to pause and reconsider how are you viewing that that sort of slightly
battered growth part of the market yeah it's been a huge hit side I think really you've got to
look for the lens of AI so if it's AI impacted I think you actually have right to be very cautious
and I think it's going to be very hard for those companies to to they're not going to re-rate it's
unlikely for them to re-rate back to where they were because the duration that they had
and the runway they had was you know very long now the risk of that is it actually is very short
because of what AI can basically build and in terms of threaten so it's really competition so
I've said quality cracks when you get competition capex and complacency and at the moment a lot of
those three and those big three things are basically hit AI you know basically hit sorry software
companies in those three ways so that's going to make it hard for those growth socks that are
software based for those growth stocks that are non software based I think you're in a better spot
so like I mentioned things like charter hall or a hub where there's either trust or it's physical
or it's a significant you know network that's where you've got a bit more a bit more of a runway
and a bit more time and that's I think where those stocks have potential to continue to deliver
and continue to perform but they still need to innovate and they still need to invest so that's
I think where the growth stocks are either challenged or all less challenged and until the market
goes through this period of uncertainty it's not going to turn aggressively towards quality I
think it'll turn to quality slowly from August onwards and then start to re-rate because it'll
start to feel more confident about the duration of these businesses but for the moment the markets
are very cautious I think rightfully so given all the uncertainties in the world well let's dive
into our three regular questions that we put to all guests that come on the podcast James could
you share a lesson from a big win or a big loss what happened and what was the lesson all right let's
go big loss first the big loss for me is after pace being one of the you know most significant
negative contributors for the fund you've got to be just willing to change your mind and this is
momentum extreme like this is in covert the market wanted to have credit available you know all
the banks were were stressing about you know jobs and pandemics and housing valuations and credit
the world was in a very difficult place in the middle of a pandemic and after pay was basically
providing short-term credit for people in the Australia market and it had a real significant
in a product that was growing very rapidly I thought regulation would come in regulation was delayed
because the market didn't want to all the government didn't want to put regulation on to credit
huh in the middle of a pandemic so that company you know went up you know 20 or 30 times from
the bottom I mean then I've obviously got then got taken over so you did know it I did know and
I actually held it at index and then thought I know this thing's not going to work and then it
went down and then it just went up you know enormously after that so yeah I think the lesson is you just
sometimes moment especially with momentum it's where the big lessons in terms of negatives
you just got to accept either you don't know or this thing has a lot of momentum and the market
has put a dream in its mind and the valuation is basically going to be extending to where that
dream is and after pay's dream was was big I mean it could be huge and that's what really happened
so that became a very significant and a 15 billion market cap and it was losing money right it
